food_name: Maggi Goreng (Fried Instant Noodles, Mamak Hawker Style)
aliases: 炒即食面 (Chǎo Jí Shí Miàn)

chinese: 炒即食面 (Chǎo Jí Shí Miàn)

food_subgroup: Stir-fried instant noodle dish with egg, vegetables, and condiment sauces

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Maggi Goreng. Closest parent is a stir-fried noodle dish. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle dishes — stir-fried noodles (closest parent category in FoodEx2 matrix)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Maggi Goreng. A00LC (noodle dishes) used as closest defensible parent. Specific mamak-style instant noodle stir-fry is not enumerated in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Maggi Goreng or mamak-style fried instant noodles.

main_ingredients: Maggi brand instant wheat noodles (NOVA Class 4 ultra-processed)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of Maggi Goreng made with 2 packets of instant noodles, weighing approximately 500g total including egg, vegetables, and sauces.

nutrient_source.title: Maggi Goreng — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo database and Nestlé Singapore Maggi instant noodle product label
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo (https://foodo.sg/) and Nestlé Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Maggi Goreng combined with Nestlé Singapore Maggi instant noodle product label data. Values represent a composite of the cooked dish (2 packets noodles + egg + vegetables + sauces) normalised to per-100g basis. HPB Foodo is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Instant Noodles — University of Sydney GI Database (refined wheat instant noodles, boiled)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 73 (high) is based on refined wheat instant noodles (boiled) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Maggi Goreng uses Maggi brand refined wheat instant noodles as its primary carbohydrate base. The stir-frying process and addition of egg, vegetables, and sauces do not substantially lower the GI of the noodle base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (73) × available carbohydrate per serving (110g) / 100 = 80.3 GL. This is one of the highest GL values per serving in Singapore hawker cuisine.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content — 480mg per 100g, equating to approximately 2,400mg sodium per standard 500g serving
hypertension.guidance: A single plate of Maggi Goreng delivers approximately 2,400mg sodium — equivalent to the WHO's entire recommended daily sodium allowance of less than 2,000mg — making it one of the highest-sodium single dishes in Singapore hawker cuisine; patients with hypertension should avoid this dish or request minimal seasoning powder and reduced sauces, and should not consume it regularly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (GI 73) and extremely high glycemic load per serving (GL ~80) from refined wheat instant noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: The two-packet serving provides approximately 110g of rapidly digestible refined wheat carbohydrate with a GI of 73 and a glycemic load of approximately 80 per serving — among the highest of any Singapore hawker dish — causing sharp postprandial glucose spikes that are particularly problematic when consumed late at night when physical activity is absent and insulin clearance is reduced; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish or substitute with a lower-GI noodle option such as fresh rice vermicelli.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (2,400mg per serving), saturated fat (12.5g per serving), and NOVA Class 4 ultra-processed food status
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Maggi Goreng is a NOVA Class 4 ultra-processed food with high sodium (2,400mg per serving), moderate saturated fat (2.5g per 100g / ~12.5g per serving), and refined carbohydrate load; regular consumption is associated with elevated cardiovascular risk through sodium-driven blood pressure elevation, atherogenic lipid effects of saturated fat, and the systemic inflammatory burden of ultra-processed food intake; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional indulgence only.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: NOVA Class 4 ultra-processed food with high sodium and refined carbohydrate — pro-inflammatory dietary pattern
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Maggi instant noodles are NOVA Class 4 ultra-processed food; ultra-processed food consumption is independently associated with higher systemic inflammatory markers; the high sodium content promotes inflammatory cytokine pathways; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption and substitute with fresh rice vermicelli stir-fry if a late-night noodle dish is desired.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maggi Goreng (炒即食面) is Singapore's most iconic mamak hawker creation: Maggi brand instant wheat noodles stir-fried in a smoking-hot wok with egg, fresh cabbage, tomato, bird's eye chilli, and a signature combination of dark soy sauce, ketchup, fish sauce, and Maggi seasoning powder. The dish is sold around the clock at mamak stalls across Singapore and Malaysia and is perhaps the quintessential Singapore late-night supper food, particularly beloved by students, shift workers, and anyone craving something hot, savoury, and filling at 1am. The noodles are first boiled and drained, then tossed in a very hot wok with oil and egg before the vegetables and sauces are added in rapid succession, producing a dish with a characteristic smoky wok-hei aroma, chewy noodle texture, and a complex sweet-salty-spicy flavour profile. From a clinical nutrition perspective, Maggi Goreng is simultaneously one of the most nutritionally challenging dishes in Singapore. A standard two-packet serving (approximately 500g) delivers approximately 825 kcal, 110g carbohydrate, 35g fat, and a staggering 2,400mg sodium — effectively the WHO's entire recommended daily sodium allowance in a single dish. The Maggi instant noodle base is a NOVA Class 4 ultra-processed food and contributes most of the sodium even before the seasoning packet is added. The glycemic index of the refined wheat noodle base is approximately 73 (high), producing a glycemic load of approximately 80 per serving — one of the highest of any Singapore hawker dish. Despite these clinical concerns, Maggi Goreng occupies a beloved and culturally irreplaceable role in Singapore's food culture; the goal is informed moderation, not elimination. Key nutrients per 100g: 165 kcal, 6g protein, 7g fat, 22g carbohydrate, 480mg sodium.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes) combined with Nestlé Singapore Maggi product label data. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database using refined wheat instant noodles as the analogous food. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI values are supported by named approved sources. The dish is a well-documented Singapore hawker staple with consistent preparation across mamak stalls.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite stir-fried dish is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite cooked dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per Nestlé Singapore Maggi product label). All core macros (protein_g 6.0g, fat_g 7.0g, carbohydrate_g 22.0g) are non-zero and fully supported. GI of 73 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat instant noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; the stir-frying process does not substantially alter the GI of the noodle base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (73) × available carbohydrate per 500g serving (110g) / 100 = 80.3. The dish is classified as NOVA Class 4 ultra-processed due to the Maggi instant noodle base. Halal status reflects standard mamak stall preparation; individual stall practices may vary. Vitamin A reported as mcg RAE estimated from egg and vegetable content. Folate reported as mcg DFE estimated from vegetable and noodle content.
